title: Security+ SY0-701 — Section 25: Application Security
description: Jason Dion Security+ SY0-701 — Section 25: Application Security. Domain: Threats, Vulnerabilities & Mitigations (2.0) & Security Architecture (3.0).

questions.question: What is the #1 risk in the OWASP Top 10 for web applications?
answers: Broken Access Control — unauthorized access to resources
SQL Injection
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
Cryptographic Failures
questions.explanation: Broken Access Control has been the #1 OWASP risk, occurring when users can act outside their intended permissions — accessing other users' data or unauthorized functions.

questions.question: What is SQL injection and how is it prevented?
answers: Inserting malicious SQL into input fields; prevented by parameterized queries and input validation
Injecting code into database files directly
Gaining physical access to database servers
Cracking database passwords using brute force
questions.explanation: SQLi attacks manipulate database queries via unsanitized input. Prevention: use parameterized queries/prepared statements, input validation, and stored procedures.

questions.question: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)?
answers: Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
Attacking users across different websites simultaneously
Stealing cookies by accessing the server
Redirecting users to malicious websites
questions.explanation: XSS attacks inject client-side scripts (usually JavaScript) into web pages that other users view, enabling attackers to steal session cookies, redirect users, or deface sites.

questions.question: What is CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)?
answers: Tricking authenticated users into unknowingly submitting malicious requests to a web app
Stealing session tokens from users
Forging SSL certificates for websites
Redirecting requests to a different server
questions.explanation: CSRF exploits the trust a web application has in an authenticated user's browser, tricking it into submitting requests (fund transfers, password changes) without the user's knowledge.

questions.question: What does 'shift left' mean in application security?
answers: Integrating security earlier in the development lifecycle rather than at the end
Moving security teams to development offices
Testing applications from left to right
Shifting security responsibilities to developers
questions.explanation: Shift left means incorporating security testing and review early in the SDLC (requirements, design, code) rather than waiting for final QA — finding issues when they're cheaper to fix.

questions.question: What is the difference between SAST and DAST?
answers: SAST analyzes source code without running it; DAST tests running applications from the outside
SAST is manual; DAST is automated
SAST tests networks; DAST tests applications
SAST is for web apps; DAST is for mobile apps
questions.explanation: Static Application Security Testing (SAST) examines source code for vulnerabilities. Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) tests running applications by simulating external attacks.

questions.question: What is a Web Application Firewall (WAF)?
answers: A filter that monitors and blocks malicious HTTP/HTTPS traffic to web applications
A firewall for protecting web servers' network connections
An encrypted proxy for web traffic
A tool for testing web application performance
questions.explanation: A WAF sits in front of web applications, analyzing HTTP/HTTPS traffic to detect and block common web attacks like SQLi, XSS, and CSRF.

questions.question: What is a buffer overflow attack?
answers: Writing more data than a buffer can hold to overwrite adjacent memory and execute arbitrary code
Overloading a server with too much data
Filling a database with excessive records
Overloading network buffers with traffic
questions.explanation: Buffer overflows occur when a program writes more data to a fixed-size buffer than it can hold, overwriting adjacent memory. Attackers exploit this to inject and execute malicious code.

questions.question: Why is input validation critical for application security?
answers: It prevents attackers from injecting malicious data through user inputs
It improves application performance
It ensures data is stored in the correct format
It prevents unauthorized API access
questions.explanation: Input validation ensures all user-supplied data is checked for type, length, format, and range before processing, preventing injection attacks, buffer overflows, and data corruption.

questions.question: What is a common API security vulnerability?
answers: Broken Object Level Authorization — accessing other users' data through API endpoint manipulation
Using REST instead of SOAP
Not using API versioning
Slow API response times
questions.explanation: BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) is the most critical API vulnerability — APIs that don't properly verify object-level permissions allow users to access others' data by changing IDs in requests.

questions.question: What is the purpose of code signing?
answers: Using a digital signature to verify the authenticity and integrity of software
Encrypting source code to protect intellectual property
Signing developer agreements for code contributions
Licensing software for distribution
questions.explanation: Code signing uses digital certificates to verify that software comes from a legitimate publisher and has not been modified since signing, protecting against tampered or malicious software.

questions.question: What is session hijacking?
answers: Stealing or predicting a valid session token to impersonate an authenticated user
Logging out a user from their session remotely
Slowing down a user's session speed
Redirecting a user's session to another server
questions.explanation: Session hijacking involves obtaining a valid session ID (via XSS, sniffing, or prediction) to impersonate a legitimate user without needing their credentials.

questions.question: What is DevSecOps?
answers: Integrating security practices throughout the DevOps pipeline (CI/CD) rather than at the end
A security team overseeing development operations
Secure deployment of DevOps tools
Development of security software using Agile
questions.explanation: DevSecOps embeds security into every phase of the DevOps pipeline — automated security testing, vulnerability scanning in CI/CD, and security reviews — making security everyone's responsibility.

questions.question: What is a race condition vulnerability?
answers: Exploiting a timing gap between security check and resource use (TOCTOU)
Competing with other users for system resources
A performance issue caused by excessive API calls
Running malware faster than security controls
questions.explanation: Race conditions (Time of Check to Time of Use / TOCTOU) occur when attackers exploit the gap between checking a condition and acting on it, manipulating the system between these two points.

questions.question: What is the STRIDE threat modeling framework?
answers: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege
Secure, Test, Review, Implement, Deploy, Evaluate
A methodology for penetration testing
A security audit framework
questions.explanation: STRIDE is Microsoft's threat categorization framework: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege — used to identify threats during design.
